content.full_text:
1/ 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Greg O'Connor, Planning Commissioner 
City of Pleasanton 
200 Old Bernal Ave 
PO Box 520 
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-046 
Dear Mr. O'Connor: 
April 7, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions 
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re 
Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete 
and accurate. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict 
of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you from participating in decisions regarding traffic flow from a 
new development near your home? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The decisions regarding traffic flow could increase the traffic through your 
neighborhood by approximately 50 percent, which will have a material financial effect on the 
interest in your property. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-15-046 
Page No. 2 
You are a Planning Commissioner for the City of Pleasanton. The Planning Commission 
will be making decisions regarding a development project that is approximately 2,500 feet from 
your home. The proposed development includes approximately 45-50 new homes and will create 
new roads for ingress and egress of the development. The Planning Commission will vote on 
whether to funnel the traffic to and from the new development through Lund Ranch Road, 
through Sunset Creek Lane, or some combination of the two. 
If the only outlet is on Lund Ranch Road, the traffic will not have access to your 
neighborhood and all traffic will bypass your neighborhood. If the only outlet is on Sunset Creek 
Lane, the traffic will be routed directly through your neighborhood, though not on your street, on 
its way to a main thoroughfare. Obviously if the decision is to split the traffic between these two 
roads, the impact on either neighborhood will be shared. 
None of the roads that are the subject of the decisions are less than 2,500 feet from your 
residence. You estimate that traffic from approximately 100 homes now filters through your 
neighborhood, passes your cul-de-sac, and makes its way to the main thoroughfare. Once 
construction is completed on the project area, an additional 50 homes could use those same 
streets. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. As a Planning Commissioner, you are a public official under the Act. (Section 
87200.) A public official has an interest in real property he owns, in which he has a direct or 
indirect interest, and in any leasehold. (Section 87103(b).) The only interest you listed is in your 
personal residence in which you have a real property interest. 
Reasonable foreseeability: 
The decisions at issue involve options for handling the traffic into and out of a new 
development project. The decisions will decide whether up to 50% more traffic will pass through 
your neighborhood once the development is complete. 
We must first determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have 
a material financial effect on your real property interest. If there is no reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect, there is no conflict. A material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is 
.. the subject of' the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) If the property is not 
explicitly involved in the decision, as is the case here, and the financial effect "can be recognized 
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." 
(Regulation 18701(b).) 


File No. A-15-046 
Page No. 3 
Factors to consider when making this determination include, among others, whether the 
public official should anticipate a financial effect on his or her economic interest as a potential 
outcome under normal circumstances when using appropriate due diligence and care and whether 
the public official has the type of economic interest that would cause a similarly situated person 
to weigh the advantages and disadvantages of the governmental decision on his or her economic 
interest in formulating a position. (See Regulation 1870l(b)(2), (b)(6) (copy enclosed).) Both of 
these factors are present here. 
Materiality 
The next inquiry is whether the foreseeable effect on the official's financial interests will 
be material. A financial effect on real property is material in several circumstances. You had 
initially inquired about the "500 foot rule," but because the project site is not within 500 feet of 
your home (even though some of the traffic could pass within 500 feet), the rule does not apply. 
Another materiality inquiry, however, states that a financial effect on real property is 
material if it: 
"Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including 
parking, of property surrounding the-official's real property parcel, 
the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any 
other factors that would affect the market value of the real property 
parcel in which the official has a financial interest." 
(Regulation 18705.2(a)(JO).) Because you have identified that, depending on the outcome of the 
decisions, the traffic could increase in your neighborhood by approximately 50 percent, this 
factor is satisfied. As such, it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed decisions could have a 
material financial effect on your real property interest. 
Neither the public generally nor the legally required exceptions appear to apply. The Act 
therefore prohibits you from participating in the related decisions. 
Additionally, Regulation 18704.3(a) (enclosed) provides that, regarding a governmental 
decision that is within or before the official's own agency, or any agency appointed by or subject 
to the budgetary control of the official's agency, the official is attempting to use his or her 
official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the 
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, 
employee or consultant of the agency. This prohibition applies to the Planning Commission and 
any agency subject to its budgetary control. The prohibition applies to the Commissioners as 
well as agency staff. 
Even if a conflict of interest is present, a public official may appear before his or her 
agency as any other member of the general public in the course of its prescribed governmental 
function to represent himself on matters related solely to his "personal interests." (Regulation 


File No. A-15-046 
Page No. 4 
18704.4(a)(2), (b)(l), (enclosed).) Such an appearance, properly made, does not constitute 
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision. An official's "personal 
interests" includes an interest in real property that is wholly owned by the official or members of 
his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18704.4(b)(])(A).) 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HMR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Heather M. Rowan 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
Enclosures: Regulation l8704.3(a) and Regulation 18704.4(a)(2), (b)(l)

new development near your home?

neighborhood by approximately 50 percent, which will have a material financial effect on the 
interest in your property.

new development near your home?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The decisions regarding traffic flow could increase the traffic through your

interest in your property.
